title: Harvard Entrance Test
seoDescription: Take a fun Harvard-style staged reasoning quiz with 80 questions across logic, patterns, numbers, evidence, and flexible thinking.
pageTitle: Only 4% Can Pass This Harvard Test
summary: A fun 80-question Harvard-style challenge built around clear reasoning, evidence, patterns, and calm problem-solving.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Harvard Entrance Test?
homepage.summary: Think you have the focus to beat 10 rounds of Ivy League-style brain teasers?

footer.aboutText: This Harvard-style test is a quick staged quiz focused on clear thinking, curiosity, careful judgement, evidence reading, and practical problem-solving. Harvard University was founded in 1636 and is widely known as the oldest institution of higher education in the United States, with a global reputation for scholarship, leadership, and rigorous discussion. This challenge uses that academic atmosphere as inspiration for an engaging self-test, not as an official Harvard University examination or admissions test.

landing.quickStartText: Easy at first. Much harder than people expect.

profiles.title: You handled the hardest rounds.
profiles.copy: Excellent reasoning across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Strong performance.
profiles.copy: You stayed sharp as the questions became more demanding. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good score.
profiles.copy: You handled the core reasoning rounds well. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep sharpening.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward calm pattern recognition and careful logic. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: First Yard Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The next round asks you to read simple evidence more carefully.

questions.prompt: Which answer best shows a clear academic reason?
choices: I agree because the evidence supports it
I agree because it sounds nice
I agree because it is popular
I agree because it is short
questions.explanation: A clear reason connects the answer to evidence.

questions.prompt: A group has 6 red cards and 4 blue cards. How many cards are there in total?
questions.explanation: 6 plus 4 equals 10.

questions.prompt: Which item is most different from the others?
questions.explanation: The first three are physical study objects. A question is an idea.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 3, 6, 9, 12, ?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 3 each time.

questions.prompt: All members of Team A wear badges. Lena is in Team A. What must be true?
choices: All badge wearers are in Team A
questions.explanation: If every Team A member wears a badge, Lena wears one.

questions.prompt: Which choice is the clearest plan?
questions.explanation: It gives a specific task and time.

questions.prompt: A student reads 5 pages each day for 4 days. How many pages is that?
questions.explanation: 5 times 4 equals 20.

questions.prompt: A door is locked before class. Which object is most likely useful?
questions.explanation: A key is normally used to open a locked door.

stageGroups.title: Evidence Starter
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice pace. Next comes a round of patterns and small comparisons.

questions.prompt: A note says, 'The new method reduced errors.' What does this mean?
questions.explanation: Reduced means there were fewer errors, not zero errors.

questions.prompt: Which statement is evidence rather than opinion?
questions.explanation: A measured score change is evidence.

questions.prompt: If 4 students share 12 markers equally, how many markers does each get?
questions.explanation: 12 divided by 4 equals 3.

questions.prompt: Which pair is most opposite?
questions.explanation: Temporary and permanent have opposite meanings.

questions.prompt: What comes next: circle, square, circle, square, ?
questions.explanation: The pattern alternates circle and square.

questions.prompt: If every valid ticket has a stamp, and this ticket has no stamp, what follows?
choices: It is not a valid ticket
questions.explanation: A valid ticket must have a stamp, so one without a stamp is not valid.

questions.prompt: A claim is popular with many students. What should you still ask?
questions.explanation: Popularity does not replace evidence.

questions.prompt: A problem seems hard because it has extra details. What should you do first?
questions.explanation: Good reasoning starts by finding the relevant information.

stageGroups.title: Pattern House
stageGroups.encouragement: You are finding the rhythm. The next stage adds more decision-making.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 5, 8, 11, ?
questions.explanation: The sequence adds 3 each time.

questions.prompt: Which number is the odd one out?
questions.explanation: 31 is odd; the others are even.

questions.prompt: A class has 20 seats. Three quarters are filled. How many seats are filled?
questions.explanation: Three quarters of 20 is 15.

questions.prompt: Book is to reading as microscope is to what?
questions.explanation: A microscope is used for observing closely.

questions.prompt: All squares are shapes. Some shapes are blue. What must be true?
questions.explanation: The first statement directly says every square is a shape.

questions.prompt: Which summary is most accurate: 'The plan is possible, but costly'?
choices: The plan may happen but has high cost
questions.explanation: Possible but costly means feasible with a cost concern.

questions.prompt: A value changes from 40 to 50. What is the increase?
questions.explanation: 50 minus 40 equals 10.

questions.prompt: A committee has two good options and limited time. What helps most?
questions.explanation: Comparing important criteria supports a reasoned choice.

stageGroups.title: Common Room Choices
stageGroups.encouragement: Good judgement. Next, the numbers and evidence start to matter more.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives the strongest cause-and-effect signal?
choices: Because the battery was empty, the device stopped
The room was bright and quiet
questions.explanation: It directly links an empty battery to the device stopping.

questions.prompt: A team earns 8 points, then loses 3, then earns 6. What is the final score?
questions.explanation: 8 minus 3 plus 6 equals 11.

questions.prompt: Which set follows the same constant-step pattern as 4, 8, 12?
questions.explanation: 4, 8, 12 adds 4 each time, and 2, 6, 10 also adds 4.

questions.prompt: Which source would best support a claim about test results?
questions.explanation: A table of scores gives direct evidence.

questions.prompt: If no quiet rooms are crowded, and Room 2 is crowded, what must be true?
questions.explanation: A crowded room cannot be one of the quiet rooms.

questions.prompt: Which option is most precise?
questions.explanation: Exactly 14 is the most precise quantity.

questions.prompt: A 60-minute session is split equally into 3 parts. How long is each part?
questions.explanation: 60 divided by 3 equals 20.

questions.prompt: A student keeps getting the same wrong result. What is the best next step?
questions.explanation: Checking the method can reveal the source of the error.

stageGroups.title: Smart Comparison
stageGroups.encouragement: Halfway there. The next round asks for sharper assumptions and cleaner logic.

questions.prompt: A report compares two plans but gives cost for only one. What is the weakness?
questions.explanation: A fair comparison needs relevant information for both plans.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 1, 4, 9, 16, ?
questions.explanation: These are square numbers.

questions.prompt: A group of 5 scores has an average of 6. What is the total?
questions.explanation: Average times count equals total, so 6 times 5 is 30.

questions.prompt: Which question best tests bias?
choices: Who benefits if this is believed?
questions.explanation: Benefits can reveal motivation or bias.

questions.prompt: Some applicants are artists. All artists submit portfolios. What follows?
questions.explanation: The applicants who are artists submit portfolios.

questions.prompt: Which phrase shows caution rather than overclaiming?
questions.explanation: May suggest a link is cautious and limited.

questions.prompt: A price rises from 80 to 100. What is the percentage increase?
questions.explanation: The increase is 20, which is 25% of 80.

questions.prompt: Two rules conflict. What is the fairest first move?
choices: Identify which rule fits the case better
questions.explanation: Fair judgement considers which rule is relevant to the case.

stageGroups.title: Assumption Lab
stageGroups.encouragement: You are past the midpoint. Next comes more layered problem solving.

questions.prompt: A study finds that practice and scores rise together. What is needed before claiming practice caused the rise?
questions.explanation: Other factors could explain the rise.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an assumption?
questions.explanation: It makes a broad claim that needs support.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 5, 10, 20, 40, ?
questions.explanation: Each number doubles.

questions.prompt: A jar has 3 green tokens and 9 yellow tokens. What fraction are green?
questions.explanation: 3 out of 12 simplifies to 1/4.

questions.prompt: Which result would most weaken a claim that a new schedule improved focus?
choices: Focus improved in a similar group without the schedule
The schedule had a clear name
questions.explanation: A similar improvement without the schedule suggests another cause.

questions.prompt: If all approved forms are signed, and Form B is approved, what must be true?
questions.explanation: Every approved form is signed.

questions.prompt: Which detail is most relevant when comparing two study methods?
choices: The same outcome measure for both
questions.explanation: Comparable outcome measures make the comparison fair.

questions.prompt: A puzzle gives too much information. Which strategy helps?
choices: Find the condition that changes the answer
questions.explanation: The key condition often determines the solution.

stageGroups.title: Applied Reasoning
stageGroups.encouragement: Strong work. The next round uses more abstract thinking.

questions.prompt: A machine triples a number and subtracts 2. If the input is 6, what is the output?
questions.explanation: 6 times 3 is 18, then subtract 2 to get 16.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion follows: All mentors give feedback. Dana is a mentor.
questions.explanation: If all mentors give feedback, Dana does too.

questions.prompt: Which pattern uses alternating operations: add 2, multiply by 2?
questions.explanation: 3 plus 2 is 5, times 2 is 10, plus 2 is 12.

questions.prompt: A claim is based on 8 people. What is the main concern?
questions.explanation: A small sample may not represent a wider group.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a counterexample?
choices: A case that challenges a general rule
A longer version of a rule
A result that repeats a rule
A number used in a table
questions.explanation: A counterexample shows that a general rule may not always hold.

questions.prompt: A line graph goes steadily upward. What does it usually show?
questions.explanation: An upward line usually indicates an increasing value.

questions.prompt: A set contains only red or blue cards. A card from the set is not red. What must it be?
questions.explanation: If only red or blue are possible, not red means blue.

questions.prompt: A team cannot solve a problem using its usual method. What is the most flexible response?
questions.explanation: Questioning an assumption can open another path.

stageGroups.title: Abstract Seminar
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent. The final two rounds are harder but still fair.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
questions.explanation: Each term is multiplied by 3.

questions.prompt: A value falls from 120 to 90. What is the decrease?
questions.explanation: 120 minus 90 equals 30.

questions.prompt: If all clear arguments have reasons, and this argument has no reasons, what follows?
choices: It is not clear by that rule
questions.explanation: A clear argument must have reasons, so one without reasons fails that condition.

questions.prompt: Which criticism best fits a study with no comparison group?
choices: It is harder to know what would have happened otherwise
questions.explanation: A comparison group helps judge what changed.

questions.prompt: A fair test compares Method A on beginners and Method B on experts. What is wrong?
choices: The groups differ before the test
questions.explanation: Different starting skill makes the comparison unfair.

questions.prompt: What is the median of 4, 8, 9, 20, 21?
questions.explanation: The middle value is 9.

questions.prompt: Which relationship matches editor to article?
questions.explanation: An editor works on an article; a builder works on a building.

questions.prompt: A question asks why a conclusion might be true, not whether it is popular. What skill is being tested?
questions.explanation: The focus is on reasons that support the conclusion.

stageGroups.title: Final Interview
stageGroups.encouragement: One last stage remains. Stay calm and use the evidence.

questions.prompt: A theory explains the evidence, but another explains it with fewer unsupported claims. Which is usually stronger?
choices: The one with fewer unsupported claims
The one with a longer name
questions.explanation: Fewer unsupported claims usually make an explanation cleaner.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 1, 2, 6, 24, 120, ?
questions.explanation: The pattern multiplies by 2, 3, 4, 5, then 6.

questions.prompt: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the chance of two heads?
questions.explanation: The outcomes are HH, HT, TH, and TT.

questions.prompt: Which statement is logically equivalent to: If a result is proven, it has evidence?
choices: If it has evidence, it is proven
If it is not proven, it has no evidence
If it is popular, it is proven
If it has no evidence, it is not proven
questions.explanation: The contrapositive is logically equivalent.

questions.prompt: Which objection best fits: 'No one complained, so everyone was satisfied'?
choices: People may be dissatisfied without complaining
questions.explanation: Silence does not guarantee satisfaction.

questions.prompt: A dataset is 3, 4, 5, 6, 40. Which measure best reduces the effect of the outlier?
questions.explanation: The median is less affected by 40.

questions.prompt: A problem has many details but only two affect the answer. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Hard questions often depend on identifying relevant information.

questions.prompt: Which answer shows intellectual humility?
choices: I am right because I answered first
Changing my mind is always weak
Evidence matters only when it agrees with me
I may revise my view if better evidence appears
questions.explanation: Intellectual humility means being willing to update a view.

questions.prompt: A conclusion depends on the claim that the sample is representative. What would most weaken it?
choices: The sample was selected from one narrow group
questions.explanation: A narrow sample may not represent the wider group.

questions.prompt: A sequence follows multiply by 2, then add 3: 4, 8, 11, 22, 25, ?
questions.explanation: After adding 3 to get 25, the next step is multiplying by 2.

questions.prompt: If 3 books cost 18 units, what do 5 books cost at the same rate?
questions.explanation: One book costs 6 units, so five cost 30.

questions.prompt: Some scholars are athletes. No athletes are absent. What must be true?
questions.explanation: The scholars who are athletes are not absent.

questions.prompt: Which hidden assumption supports: 'The seminar should be longer because students asked more questions'?
choices: More questions show useful demand for discussion
questions.explanation: The argument assumes more questions indicate value in more discussion.

questions.prompt: A result repeats across several independent trials. What does that usually increase?
questions.explanation: Repeated independent results usually strengthen confidence.

questions.prompt: Two values have the same mean but different spread. Which concept describes the spread?
questions.explanation: Range describes the distance between low and high values.

questions.prompt: When two good principles conflict, what is the best response?
choices: Choose the older principle every time
Weigh their relevance to the case
questions.explanation: Conflicting principles require careful weighing.
